medications.drug_name: Oral sodium chloride / dietary salt supplementation
medications.indication_rationale: Increased sodium intake (guideline-suggested up to ~10 g/day in POTS, if not contraindicated) expands intravascular volume and reduces the postural HR rise. The source note recommends aggressive salt loading; formal salt tablets are a specialist-guided option and any specific dose should be set at in-person review.
contraindications_in_this_case: Would be contraindicated if hypertension, heart failure or renal impairment were present — none reported here, but should be confirmed at in-person review before high-dose salt loading

medications.indication_rationale: A volume-expanding mineralocorticoid used in POTS when conservative measures (fluid, salt, compression, exercise) are insufficient. It is a specialist-guided decision after arrhythmia exclusion — NOT to be initiated on a first telehealth contact. No dose is specified because the source note names it only as a future/specialist option.
contraindications_in_this_case: Requires monitoring of blood pressure and potassium; not to be started before formal diagnosis and specialist input

medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose beta-blockade can blunt the postural tachycardia in POTS, but only after a tachyarrhythmia has been excluded with an ECG and under specialist guidance. The source note lists beta-blockers as a potential future therapy, not a current prescription. No dose is given because none is specified in the source.
contraindications_in_this_case: Must not be started remotely before an ECG excludes an arrhythmia and postural BP is confirmed maintained

medications.indication_rationale: A rate-controlling alternative that lowers sinus rate without affecting blood pressure, used in POTS/IST under specialist direction. Listed as an alternative option only; not a first-contact telehealth prescription.

medications.drug_name: Benzodiazepine / anxiolytic as primary treatment for presumed anxiety
medications.indication_rationale: Prescribing an anxiolytic on the assumption this is a panic/anxiety disorder is the anchoring-trap error of commission: it treats a misdiagnosis, does nothing for the underlying dysautonomia, and delays the ECG and orthostatic testing that exclude a dangerous arrhythmia.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Aggressive fluid loading — aim for roughly 2-3 litres of water daily — to expand blood volume and reduce the postural heart-rate spike.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Increasing intravascular volume is a first-line non-drug measure in POTS and directly reduces orthostatic intolerance.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Substantially increase dietary salt intake (guideline range up to ~10 g/day if not contraindicated), confirmed safe at in-person review.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Sodium promotes fluid retention and volume expansion, a core conservative therapy for POTS.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Put on high-grade, waist-high compression garments BEFORE getting out of bed each morning.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Lower-body and abdominal compression prevents venous pooling on standing, blunting the reflex tachycardia and allowing safer mobilisation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use physical counter-manoeuvres (leg crossing, calf muscle pumping, tensing) when standing, and begin a graded recumbent-based reconditioning program (e.g. rowing/recumbent cycling) as tolerated.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Counter-manoeuvres acutely support venous return; graded exercise reconditioning is a cornerstone of longer-term POTS management.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a shower chair and avoid prolonged standing in hot environments such as hot showers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Heat causes vasodilation that worsens venous pooling and presyncope; seated showering removes a high-risk fall situation.

patient_education_points.point: Your symptoms are triggered by gravity: when you stand, blood pools in your legs (the purple colour), and your heart races to keep blood flowing to your brain, causing the dizziness and shakiness. Lying down fixes it because it restores blood flow.

This looks like a disorder of the automatic nervous system rather than a heart-rhythm problem, but we need an in-person check with an ECG and a standing test to be sure and to rule out a rhythm disturbance before starting any heart medication.
It is a real physical condition, not anxiety — although the racing heart can make you feel anxious. It is usually manageable with fluids, salt, compression and gradual conditioning.
Practical steps today: drink extra salty fluids, do not stand up in the shower (use a chair), and get waist-high compression tights to put on before you get out of bed.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment for ECG and active stand testing; then GP review within 1-2 weeks for a cardiology / autonomic specialist referral
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Active stand test with paired BP/HR, 12-lead ECG, response to conservative measures, hydration and functional status; arrange specialist referral and consider secondary-cause bloods.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any true fainting/collapse, a racing heart that persists while lying flat, new chest pain, or inability to keep fluids down — seek emergency care rather than waiting.

must_include_items: Recognise a strictly postural, immediately-supine-relieved tachycardia pattern as orthostatic intolerance / a likely dysautonomia rather than anxiety
Keep a paroxysmal tachyarrhythmia on the differential and arrange an ECG to exclude it
Arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment for active stand testing and postural vitals that cannot be done over chat
Advise first-line conservative measures: increased fluids, increased salt, compression garments and seated showering
Give clear safety-netting for true syncope, rest/supine tachycardia, chest pain or dehydration
acceptable_alternatives: Routing via urgent care rather than a same-day GP for the ECG and active stand test
A prompt (within 24-48 hour) in-person review if same-day is genuinely unavailable, provided robust syncope safety-netting is given
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange in-person assessment / ECG and leaving the patient on a purely remote plan
Not excluding a tachyarrhythmia before attributing symptoms to dysautonomia
Omitting conservative first-line advice (fluids, salt, compression)
errors_of_commission: Attributing the presentation to a panic/anxiety disorder and prescribing an anxiolytic
Starting a beta-blocker, fludrocortisone or ivabradine remotely before an ECG and formal diagnosis
Over-triaging a haemodynamically stable patient to an emergency ambulance (000)
Ordering an echocardiogram or extensive cardiac imaging as an unfocused first step
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES both (a) arranging an in-person assessment with an ECG to exclude an arrhythmia and (b) first-line conservative advice. Closing the case remotely as anxiety, or starting cardiac medication without an ECG, is a critical failure.
